topic.name: Partnership
topic.description: Profit-sharing among partners based on capital invested and time period, including working-partner salary adjustments. Estimated reading 20 min; practice 35 min.

theory.introduction: Partnership problems deal with dividing business profit (or loss) among partners in proportion to their effective investment. Effective investment equals capital multiplied by the time it remains in the business. When partners join or leave at different times, or when one partner receives a salary for managing the firm, the profit must be adjusted before sharing.
theory.explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

In a simple partnership, all partners invest capital for the same duration. Profit is shared in the ratio of their capitals: A's share : B's share = A's capital : B's capital.

In a compound partnership, partners invest for different periods. Compare capitals after weighting by time: Effective capital = Capital × Time (months or years). Profit ratio = C1T1 : C2T2 : C3T3.

When a working partner gets a fixed monthly or annual salary, subtract total salary from gross profit first; divide the remainder among all partners (including the working partner) in the capital-time ratio.

If a partner joins mid-year, count only the months they actually invested. Replacements are handled by treating each investment period separately.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Startups split equity based on funding rounds, joint ventures allocate returns among investors with different lock-in periods, and family businesses distribute annual profit among sleeping and active partners using these same ratio rules.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Ignoring time when capitals are invested for unequal durations.
2) Including salary in profit before deducting it.
3) Using revenue instead of profit to divide.
4) Forgetting to convert all investments to the same time unit (months vs years).
5) Adding capitals without forming a ratio first.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Profit-sharing ratio uses capital × time, not capital alone when times differ.
• Salary to a working partner is an expense, not part of their capital share.
• Loss is shared in the same ratio as profit unless stated otherwise.
• When capital is withdrawn partly, split the partner's contribution into periods.
• Always simplify the ratio before calculating individual shares.

formulas.title: Simple partnership ratio
formulas.content: Profit ratio = C1 : C2 : C3 (when investment time is equal)

formulas.title: Compound partnership ratio
formulas.content: Profit ratio = C1 × T1 : C2 × T2 : C3 × T3

formulas.title: Share of a partner
formulas.content: Partner's share = Total profit × (Partner's ratio part / Sum of ratio parts)

formulas.title: Working partner adjustment
formulas.content: Distributable profit = Gross profit − Total salary of working partner(s)

formulas.title: Effective capital
formulas.content: Effective capital = Capital invested × Number of months (or years) invested

shortcutTips.title: Same time shortcut
shortcutTips.tip: If every partner invests for the full year, compare capitals directly without multiplying by time.

shortcutTips.title: Salary first
shortcutTips.tip: Always subtract the working partner's salary from total profit before applying the capital ratio.

shortcutTips.title: Month alignment
shortcutTips.tip: Convert every investment period to months to avoid mixing years and months in the ratio.

shortcutTips.title: Ratio simplify
shortcutTips.tip: Divide all capital-time products by their HCF before calculating shares — smaller numbers reduce errors.

shortcutTips.title: Mid-year entry
shortcutTips.tip: A partner joining after m months in a 12-month year uses time = (12 − m) months for that period.

shortcutTips.title: Check total
shortcutTips.tip: Individual shares must add up to the distributable profit; use this to verify your answer quickly.

solvedExamples.problem: A and B start a business with ₹8,000 and ₹12,000 respectively. At the end of the year the profit is ₹5,000. Find A's share.
A invests ₹15,000 for 8 months and B invests ₹12,000 for 10 months. If the profit is ₹1,550, find B's share.
A, B, and C invest ₹20,000, ₹30,000, and ₹50,000. C is a working partner and gets ₹2,000 salary per year. Annual profit is ₹12,000. Find C's total earnings.
solvedExamples.solution: C receives ₹7,000 in total (₹5,000 profit share + ₹2,000 salary).

solvedExamples.problem: X puts ₹6,000 for 4 months, Y puts ₹8,000 for 6 months, and Z puts ₹10,000 for 8 months. Profit is ₹3,720. Find Y's share.

exercise.title: General Questions
exercise.description: MCQ practice on simple and compound partnership, profit division, and working-partner salary problems.

questions.question: A and B invest ₹4,000 and ₹6,000 in a business for one year. If the profit is ₹2,500, what is A's share?
Three partners invest ₹10,000, ₹15,000, and ₹25,000 for equal time. Profit is ₹9,000. The largest share is:
P invests ₹8,000 for 12 months and Q invests ₹6,000 for 8 months. The ratio of their profit shares is:
A puts ₹12,000 for 9 months and B puts ₹16,000 for 6 months. Profit is ₹1,700. B's share is:
A and B invest in ratio 3:5. Total profit is ₹4,800. How much more does B receive than A?
X, Y, and Z invest ₹5,000, ₹7,500, and ₹12,500 for one year. Profit is ₹5,000. Y's share is:
A invests ₹20,000 for 6 months. B joins with ₹30,000 for the remaining 6 months. Profit at year end is ₹4,400. A's share is:
A working partner gets ₹1,000 per month as salary. Annual profit is ₹45,000 and capital ratio of A to B is 2:3. B's profit share (excluding A's salary) is:
M and N enter with ₹18,000 and ₹12,000. After 4 months, O joins with ₹24,000. Profit after one year is ₹9,660. O's share is:
A and B start with ₹25,000 each. After 6 months, C joins with ₹50,000. Annual profit is ₹18,000. C's share is:
questions.explanation: Effective capital of each = 300000. Equal shares. C = 18000/3 = ₹6,000.

questions.question: A, B, and C invest ₹6,000, ₹9,000, and ₹15,000. After 6 months A withdraws ₹2,000. Profit at year end is ₹4,840. B's share is:
Two partners share profit in ratio 4:6. If the smaller share is ₹2,400, the total profit is:
A and B invest ₹50,000 and ₹75,000. After 8 months, A withdraws half his capital and B adds ₹25,000. Profit after one year is ₹8,280. A's share is:
In a partnership, A's capital is double B's capital. If total profit is ₹3,600, B's share is:
P invests ₹10,000 for 10 months and Q invests ₹15,000 for 6 months. Profit is ₹1,900. P's share is:
A, B, and C share profit in ratio 5:7:8. If C receives ₹4,800, the total profit is:
A and B enter a partnership sharing profit equally. A invests ₹16,000 for 8 months. B invests for 12 months. B's capital is:
Three partners A, B, C invest capitals in ratio 2:3:4. If B's share of ₹4,500 profit is fixed, total profit is:
A and B start a business. A invests ₹30,000 for 12 months and B invests ₹45,000 for 8 months. If profit is ₹7,000, A's share is:
questions.explanation: Effective capital of both = 360000. Shares are equal. A's share = 7000/2 = ₹3,500.

questions.question: A, B, C invest ₹40,000, ₹60,000, and ₹80,000. C manages the business and gets 10% of profit as salary plus capital share. Profit is ₹18,000. C's total amount is:
questions.explanation: Salary = ₹1,800. Distributable = 16200. Ratio 2:3:4. C's profit share = 7200. Total = 7200 + 1800 = ₹9,000.

questions.question: X invests ₹35,000 and Y invests ₹45,000 for 6 months. After 6 months, X adds ₹5,000 more. At year end profit is ₹4,290. Y's share is:
A and B share losses in ratio 3:4. If A's loss is ₹1,800, total loss is:
A invests ₹5,000 for 6 months, B ₹6,000 for 5 months, and C ₹4,000 for 8 months. Profit is ₹1,380. A's share is:
Two partners started a business. First partner's capital was ₹20,000 for full year. Second partner joined after 3 months with ₹30,000. Profit is ₹5,667. Second partner's share is:
questions.explanation: P1 effective = 240000, P2 effective = 270000. Ratio 8:9. P2's share = 5667 × 9/17 = ₹3,000.

questions.question: A, B, and C invest ₹12,000, ₹18,000, and ₹30,000 for equal periods. If total profit is ₹9,200 and C also receives ₹1,200 salary, C's total earnings are:
questions.explanation: Distributable profit = 9200 − 1200 = 8000. Ratio 2:3:5. C's profit share = 4000. Total earnings = 4000 + 1200 = ₹5,200.
